one_line_takeaway: SPARSE defends text embeddings against inversion attacks (Vec2text, GEIA, MLC) by learning which dimensions encode a user-specified privacy concept and injecting theoretically-grounded anisotropic (Mahalanobis) DP noise concentrated on those dimensions, cutting leakage substantially more than uniform-noise DP baselines (e.g., STS12 epsilon=10: 19.31% vs 22.34%/22.66% Leakage) while better preserving downstream utility, at the acknowledged cost that protection is scoped to explicitly predefined concepts.
key_finding: On STS12 at epsilon=10, SPARSE reduces Leakage from the unprotected baseline of 60.09% to 19.31%, versus LapMech's 22.34% and PurMech's 22.66% (the paper states baselines achieve 'only a 22% reduction' vs SPARSE's larger drop), while SPARSE also achieves higher downstream utility (65.27%) than LapMech/PurMech (60.72% both) at that epsilon. Across attack models on STS12 at epsilon=5, SPARSE reduces leakage by 92.78% (Vec2text), 72.06% (GEIA), and 66.86% (MLC) relative to unprotected embeddings, versus smaller reductions for LapMech/PurMech (e.g., 87.75%/87.65% for Vec2text, 51.46%/51.22% for GEIA, 63.55%/62.78% for MLC), with the paper noting shallow MLC is least affected by any defense. On real-world data at epsilon=10, SPARSE reduces MIMIC-III 'sex' attribute leakage from an unprotected 88.40% to 28.45%, versus LapMech/PurMech's 43.38%/43.38% (both baselines identical to 2 d.p.); across all PII-300K/MIMIC-III categories tested (Sex, City, State, Country, Age, Disease, Symptom) SPARSE consistently shows the lowest leakage of the three defenses. The white-box variant SPARSE-WB (Table 4) outperforms SPARSE (e.g., STS12 epsilon=5: Leakage 1.43% vs SPARSE's 4.34% vs LapMech's 7.36%), but the gap narrows substantially at higher epsilon (20,30,40), which the authors interpret as SPARSE closely approximating the ideal white-box sensitivity estimate without attacker access. A qualitative analysis (Fig. 2, Table 5) shows learned neuron masks cluster along linguistically coherent groups (weekdays, countries, months, gender terms) and that protecting one target token's dimensions also reduces leakage for semantically similar but unprotected tokens (e.g., -76.2% leakage for the target 'Weekdays' concept vs -46.2% for semantically similar unprotected weekday tokens vs -11.7% for unrelated tokens).
core_contribution: The paper introduces SPARSE (Sensitivity-guided Privacy-Aware Representations for better SEmantic-preserving), a user-centric local differential privacy (LDP) defense for text embeddings against embedding inversion attacks. It has two components: (1) a differentiable neuron mask learning module (using a hard-concrete/Gumbel-softmax relaxation, Louizos et al. 2018 style L0 regularization) that learns which embedding dimensions are 'privacy-sensitive' for a user-specified concept C (e.g. age, sex, disease), trained via a positive/negative sentence pair contrastive objective (sentences containing concept tokens vs. the same sentences with those tokens removed); and (2) a novel 'Mahalanobis mechanism,' an extension of the generalized Laplace mechanism (Wu et al., 2017) that injects anisotropic ('elliptical') noise calibrated by the learned dimension-sensitivity mask (as a diagonal covariance matrix), rather than uniform spherical noise. The authors prove the Mahalanobis mechanism satisfies epsilon-metric-LDP under the Mahalanobis norm (Theorem 1, proved via triangle inequality) and show this norm is asymptotically equivalent (up to constants sqrt(n) and sqrt(c)) to the standard Euclidean-norm LDP guarantee of the generalized Laplace mechanism (Lemmas 1-2). They also propose SPARSE-WB, a white-box variant using Integrated Gradients feature attribution instead of the learned mask, as an empirical upper bound.
threat_model: Local differential privacy setting: each user perturbs their own text embedding before sharing it (e.g., before storage in a vector DB / RAG system), so there is no trusted aggregator. The adversary is an embedding inversion attacker with access to the (obfuscated) embedding Φ'(s) who tries to recover sensitive tokens/attributes belonging to a user-defined privacy concept C (e.g., names, medical conditions, age, sex, city). Three attack models are used to instantiate this adversary: Vec2text (Morris et al., 2023, sentence-level LLM-based reconstruction, used as the default attack because of its strength), GEIA (Li et al., 2023, generative sentence-level inversion), and MLC (Song & Raghunathan, 2020, a shallow 3-layer MLP predicting presence of individual words, token-level). The black-box SPARSE defense assumes no access to the attacker's internals when learning which dimensions to protect (mask learned purely from D+/D- sentence pairs); SPARSE-WB is an explicit white-box variant that assumes access to the attack model's gradients via Integrated Gradients, serving as an upper-bound comparison rather than the paper's realistic deployment setting.
limitations: The paper's own Ethical Considerations section states: (1) SPARSE reduces but does not eliminate inversion risk and could create a false sense of security if practitioners over-rely on it instead of broader data governance/access controls; (2) the framework is concept-driven and depends on the user predefining sensitive categories — attributes not included in the specified concept space may receive materially less protection (the paper's own Table 5 shows 'Other'/unrelated tokens get only 11.7%-29.1% leakage reduction versus 61-76% for the explicitly targeted concept), raising fairness concerns for un-anticipated sensitive attributes; (3) as a general-purpose privacy technique, it could be misused to mask malicious content from moderation systems. Additionally, the shallow MLC attack model is noted as 'less vulnerable' to the defense than deeper attacks (Vec2text, GEIA), meaning the marginal privacy gain over baselines shrinks for weaker/simpler adversaries. The white-box comparison (SPARSE-WB) shows there remains a real performance gap at low epsilon between the practical black-box SPARSE and the theoretical upper bound. The reproducibility statement notes code was not yet released at submission time ('we plan to publicly release our code in the near future').

datasets_used: STS12 (SemEval, STS task, 10684 sentences, 123 unique named entities)
FIQA (Financial, Retrieval task, 5500 sentences, 41 unique named entities)
STSB (SemEval, STS task, 17256 sentences, 228 unique named entities)
STS14 (SemEval, STS task, 3000 sentences, 41 unique named entities)
Quora (QA, Retrieval task, 10000 sentences, 90 unique named entities)
NFCorpus (Medical, Retrieval task, 2590 sentences, 13 unique named entities)
MIMIC-III (Medical clinical notes, 4244 sentences, 290 unique named entities)
PII-Masking-300K (PII domain, 177677 sentences, 491 unique named entities, 27 PII types)
quantitative_results: STS12, epsilon=10: Leakage - LapMech 22.34%, PurMech 22.66%, SPARSE 19.31%; Downstream utility - LapMech 60.72%, PurMech 60.72%, SPARSE 65.27%
STS12, epsilon=5, Vec2text attack: Leakage reduction from unprotected 60.09% - LapMech -87.75%, PurMech -87.65%, SPARSE -92.78%
STS12, epsilon=5, GEIA attack: Leakage reduction from unprotected 25.34% - LapMech -51.46%, PurMech -51.22%, SPARSE -72.06%
STS12, epsilon=5, MLC attack: Leakage reduction from unprotected 53.20% - LapMech -63.55%, PurMech -62.78%, SPARSE -66.86%
MIMIC-III, epsilon=10, Sex attribute: unprotected 88.40%, LapMech 43.38%, PurMech 43.38%, SPARSE 28.45%
MIMIC-III, epsilon=10, Disease attribute: unprotected 70.43%, LapMech 23.32%, PurMech 22.86%, SPARSE 18.28%
PII-300K, epsilon=10, Sex attribute: unprotected 86.12%, LapMech 42.35%, PurMech 43.53%, SPARSE 33.76%
STS12 white-box comparison, epsilon=5: Leakage - LapMech 7.36%, SPARSE 4.34%, SPARSE-WB 1.43%; Downstream utility - LapMech 29.28%, SPARSE 34.12%, SPARSE-WB 40.92%
Qualitative leakage mitigation at epsilon=10 by token category (Table 5): Weekdays target -76.2%, semantically similar -46.2%, unrelated -11.7%; Gender target -61.0%, similar -40.5%, unrelated -18.3%
Appendix A: privacy-neuron sensitivity distribution, top-10% vs bottom-10% dimensions, mean sensitivity ~0.04 for top group, Wilcoxon signed-rank test p=1.30e-21
baselines_compared: LapMech: generalized Laplace mechanism (Wu et al., 2017) — isotropic/spherical Laplace noise
PurMech: Purkayastha mechanism (Du et al., 2023) — directional noise for embedding sanitization
SPARSE-WB: white-box variant using Integrated Gradients attribution instead of the learned mask, used as an empirical upper bound, not a prior baseline from other work
